disclaimer: PaxStressia is grounded in real counterinsurgency scholarship and the documented Sahel crisis of 2012–2023, then diverges into alt-history. Insurgent factions are composites modeled on real groups, not the groups themselves. Entries below are flagged: VERIFIED (checked against reporting), ESTABLISHED (canonical scholarship/record), or ALT-HISTORY (the game's projection beyond the reliable record — not presented as fact). The game has a point of view; it does not invent its evidence.

entries.citation: Gil Merom, How Democracies Lose Small Wars (Cambridge University Press, 2003).
entries.note: The game's central thesis: democracies lose counterinsurgencies at home, when domestic tolerance for cost and brutality collapses before the insurgency does. This is the Domestic gauge and the Mandate coupling.

entries.citation: David Galula, Counterinsurgency Warfare: Theory and Practice (1964).
entries.note: COIN is ~80% political, 20% military; the population is the prize. This is why Local legitimacy, not attrition, is the win condition.

entries.citation: U.S. Army / Marine Corps, FM 3-24 Counterinsurgency (2006).
entries.note: Legitimacy as the objective, and the paradoxes ('the more force you use, the less effective you may be') — the law behind every initiative having a backfire channel.

entries.citation: David Kilcullen, The Accidental Guerrilla (Oxford University Press, 2009).
entries.note: Heavy-handed presence creates insurgents; occupation past a welcome threshold generates antibodies. The occupation-duration decay on Local legitimacy.

entries.citation: Ben Connable & Martin Libicki, How Insurgencies End (RAND, 2010); Paul, Clarke, Grill, Paths to Victory (RAND, 2013).
entries.note: Sanctuaries and external sponsors are the decisive survival variables; leadership decapitation rarely ends an insurgency; insurgencies average ~a decade. Why edges matter more than nodes, and decapitation is deliberately weak.

entries.citation: Mali coups (Aug 2020, May 2021); Burkina Faso coups (Jan 2022, Sept 2022); Niger coup (July 2023).
entries.note: The coup cascade the scenario reproduces — each junta claiming only it could defeat the insurgency. The collapse-roll's junta path.

entries.citation: Alliance of Sahel States, founded 16 Sept 2023 (Liptako-Gourma Charter); Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger; announced ECOWAS withdrawal Jan 2024.
entries.note: An authoritarian bloc manufactured by insurgency pressure, aligned with a rival patron — the bloc-consolidation clock.

entries.citation: Wagner Group deployment to Mali (from ~Dec 2021), later rebranded 'Africa Corps' under Russian state control.
entries.note: The mercenary patron: coup-proofing with no human-rights strings, paid in mining concessions — the allegiance market's no-strings offer.

entries.citation: Moura massacre, central Mali, 27–31 March 2022. UN/OHCHR fact-finding (report 12 May 2023): 500+ killed by Malian armed forces and 'foreign military personnel' (widely identified as Wagner); 58 women and girls subjected to sexual violence.
entries.note: The atrocity that quiet hides — the partner-force and errant-strike events, and the Transparency Dial.

entries.citation: International Crisis Group and ACLED analyses of the Sahel: falling attack counts can reflect insurgent entrenchment into local political and economic life (shadow taxation, courts), not improved security.
entries.note: Pillar 4 (quiet ≠ peace): the Visibility axis, deceptive-calm fog, and the insurgent-grip discount in scoring.

entries.citation: Several Sahel coup leaders were Western-trained officers (widely reported across the 2020–2023 coups).
entries.note: Train-and-equip blowback: the coup-seed backfire on the Train & Equip initiative.

entries.citation: AES deepening (joint force, common-currency plans, party dissolutions) and 2024–2026 events depicted in the scenario.
entries.note: ALT-HISTORY: the scenario projects the trajectory past the reliable record. Treated as the game's extrapolation from documented trends, not as established fact. Confirm specifics before citing them as history.

entries.citation: Council on Foreign Relations — Global Conflict Tracker.
entries.note: Conflict-status reference used to set starting conditions.

entries.citation: ACLED — Armed Conflict Location & Event Data Project.
entries.note: Incident-level data informing regional contestation and the tri-border focus.

entries.citation: International Crisis Group — Sahel reporting.
entries.note: Field analysis behind the central-Mali and Burkina spillover modeling.

entries.citation: Freedom House — Freedom in the World; Transnational Repression reports.
entries.note: The closed-society and exposure layer (§20): transnational repression as a documented pattern.

entries.citation: Rebel Inc. (Ndemic Creations / James Vaughan, 2018).
entries.note: Reputation, intel fog, civ/mil balance, negotiated endings — the closest design precedent. PaxStressia splits its single reputation into three legitimacies.

entries.citation: GMT Games COIN series, designed by Volko Ruhnke (Fire in the Lake, 2014; A Distant Plain).
entries.note: Existence proof that asymmetric COIN is fun turn-based; asymmetry of goals and dual-use event cards.

entries.citation: Twilight Struggle (Ananda Gupta & Jason Matthews, GMT Games, 2005).
entries.note: Global influence at node scale, two-bloc tug — the map's scale model.

entries.citation: Afghanistan '11 (Every Single Soldier / Slitherine, 2017). Removed from Apple's iOS App Store in 2018 for depicting 'a specific government or other real entity as the enemies.'
entries.note: The cautionary platform tale driving the distribution order (web → Android → Steam/itch → iOS last).

entries.citation: Procedural-persona / self-play automated game balancing (MCTS personas; iterative tuning for asymmetric games).
entries.note: The policy archetypes and the Monte-Carlo + history-calibration harness — the design's claims asserted as regression tests.

entries.citation: EEAS–VIGINUM–FCDO joint technical report on the 'African Initiative', June 2025; EUvsDisinfo.
entries.note: A Moscow news agency built on former Wagner assets running coordinated FIMI across the Sahel; with the April 2022 Gossi staged-corpse operation, grounds the 'closed_fimi' card.

entries.citation: Human Rights Watch and Amnesty International reporting, 2023–2026: party dissolution (Mali 2024, Burkina Faso 2026) and Burkina Faso's April 2023 mobilization decree.
entries.note: Repression by decree and the punitive conscription of critics; grounds 'closed_decree' and 'closed_conscription'.

entries.citation: Committee to Protect Journalists and Reporters Without Borders, 2023–2024: France 24, RFI and BBC suspensions/bans across Mali, Burkina Faso and Niger.
entries.note: The escalating 'media-ban ladder'; grounds 'closed_blackout'.

entries.citation: UN Security Council Res. 2690 (June 2023) terminating MINUSMA; Russia's veto of the Mali sanctions Panel of Experts, August 2023 (UN News; HRW).
entries.note: The end of independent oversight; grounds 'closed_panel'.

entries.citation: Human Rights Watch, 'US Lifts Sanctions on Wagner-Linked Officials in Mali', March 2026; The Sentry, on Wagner→Africa Corps.
entries.note: Accountability reversed for realpolitik — the sobering counterweight; grounds 'closed_reversal'.
